perturbation.original_text: 4 - The Receiving Party undertakes during the term of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after the date of its expiry or termination: NDA – EUNCL & Sponsor 4/6 - to safeguard Confidential Information as it does for its own proprietary information of like importance, but at least with a reasonable degree of care, - to use Confidential Information only for the Purpose and to divulge Confidential Information to its personnel for said Purpose only and on a "need to know" basis, - not to duplicate or otherwise reproduce Confidential Information except for such copies as the Receiving Party may require for the Purpose as aforesaid, provided that all copies shall contain the same proprietary and confidential notices and legends as appear on the original Confidential Information, - not to divulge Confidential Information to any third party for any purpose, unless and until expressly authorized in writing to do so by the Disclosing Party, - not to reverse-compile, reverse-assemble or reverse-engineer Confidential Information or any part thereof, unless directed to do so by the disclosing Party as proven by appropriate documentation - not to claim nor to register any Intellectual Property right, nor to exercise any Intellectual Property right or any other right on Confidential Information received under the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text:
7 - The Confidential Information is disclosed, “as is”. The Disclosing Party makes no representations or warranties, whether express or implied, with respect to Confidential Information, and notably their fitness for a commercial technical purpose. The use of Confidential Information is made at the sole risk of the Receiving Party.

4 - The Receiving Party undertakes during the term of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after the date of its expiry or termination: NDA – EUNCL & Sponsor 4/6 - to safeguard Confidential Information as it does for its own proprietary information of like importance, but at least with a reasonable degree of care, - to use Confidential Information only for the Purpose and to divulge Confidential Information to its personnel for said Purpose only and on a "need to know" basis, - not to duplicate or otherwise reproduce Confidential Information except for such copies as the Receiving Party may require for the Purpose as aforesaid, provided that all copies shall contain the same proprietary and confidential notices and legends as appear on the original Confidential Information, - not to divulge Confidential Information to any third party for any purpose, unless and until expressly authorized in writing to do so by the Disclosing Party, - not to reverse-compile, reverse-assemble or reverse-engineer Confidential Information or any part thereof, unless directed to do so by the disclosing Party as proven by appropriate documentation - not to claim nor to register any Intellectual Property right, nor to exercise any Intellectual Property right or any other right on Confidential Information received under the Agreement. 8 - The Agreement is personal to the Parties (« intuitu personae ») and the Receiving Party undertakes not to assign nor transfer its rights or obligations under the Agreement to any third party, including an affiliated company, without the Disclosing Party’s prior written approval.
perturbation.explanation: Reordering the placement of the 'as is' disclosure so that it appears at the top of the section concerning the Receiving Party's confidentiality obligations creates confusion, making it seem like the Receiving Party must accept the confidential information in its current state (with no guarantee).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 1134 of the Belgian Civil Code
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1134 of the Belgian Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: This change implies a disclaimer of responsibility for the Confidential Information, impacting good faith and the balance of obligations outlined in Article 1134 of the Belgian Civil Code, which dictates agreements must be executed in good faith. The 'as is' disclaimer can be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent liability regarding the quality or accuracy of the confidential information, potentially violating the principle of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The placement of the "as is" disclosure at the beginning of the section outlining the Receiving Party's confidentiality obligations introduces a structural flaw. The Law Explanation suggests that this disclaimer attempts to circumvent liability regarding the quality or accuracy of the confidential information, potentially violating the principle of good faith. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction,' where a required legal disclosure is placed in a misleading section, making it difficult for parties to interpret. The provided snippet from the French Civil Code, specifically referencing Article 1134 concerning the effect of obligations, supports the idea that agreements must be executed in good faith. Placing the disclaimer upfront could be interpreted as acting in bad faith, thus creating a contradiction.
